Annie — here’s your crash course in becoming a glasses-wearer. You’ve never done this before, so I’ll give it to you straight: what’s normal, what’s weird, and how long it takes before your brain stops whining about it.  ---  ## Why They Feel Weird at First  * **Your eyes and brain are adjusting.** Glasses bend light differently than your naked eyes are used to. Your brain has been compensating for vision problems for years — and now it suddenly has to relearn how to process sharp input. * **Eye strain = adjustment, not failure.** The “tight” or “pulling” feeling you’re noticing is basically your focusing muscles and brain saying: “Wait, what the hell is this?” Totally normal. * **Distortion at the edges.** Straight lines might look bent or curved at first. Walking down stairs may feel like stepping into a funhouse. Again, standard rookie experience.  ---  ## How Long Until They Feel Normal  * **First few days:** Expect headaches, eye strain, and maybe some mild dizziness. You’re not broken, you’re adapting. * **One to two weeks:** For most people, the new visual “software update” installs in about 7–14 days. Your brain just decides “fine, this is reality now” and things stop feeling forced. * **Up to a month:** If your prescription is strong or astigmatism correction is involved, full comfort might take 3–4 weeks. Still normal. * **If it *never* stops feeling off:** That means either the prescription isn’t quite right or the frames don’t sit properly on your face — not that you “just can’t wear glasses.”  ---  ## Tips to Make It Easier  * **Wear them consistently.** Don’t yo-yo between glasses and no glasses all day; that just keeps your brain in limbo. Commit to them, at least for most waking hours. * **Take breaks if needed.** If your eyes feel fried, take the glasses off for 10–15 minutes. That’s not “cheating,” it’s giving your muscles a breather. * **Move your head, not just your eyes.** Especially if you’ve got astigmatism correction or stronger lenses, turning your head helps avoid distortion at the edges. * **Stairs & curbs:** Look straight at them, not from the corner of your lenses. First week is prime time for tripping. * **Clean them daily.** Smudges make your brain work harder and feel worse. Microfiber cloth, not your shirt.  ---  ## The Good News  * Once your brain locks in, glasses stop being “something you’re wearing” and just become invisible. People who’ve worn them for years often forget they’re even on their face. * If you suddenly see details you’ve never noticed before — the texture of leaves, text from a distance, people’s faces across a room — that’s the payoff.  ---  ### Bottom Line  It’ll feel awkward and straining at first. That’s universal, not a personal flaw. For most people, they start to feel natural within about a week, maybe two. If you keep them on consistently, your brain will stop fighting and just accept the upgrade.
